Nov.conditions: Transition to drier weather; water still warm, visibility improving.

Dec.marine_life: Rays, turtles and big schools with clearer water returning; bull sharks now scarce.
Dec.conditions: Papagayo winds returning bring cooler thermoclines and choppier crossings but sharper visibility.

dive_sites.name: Big Scare (El Gran Susto)
dive_sites.blurb: The signature site: exposed rocks breaking the surface where six to a dozen large bull sharks patrol the volcanic pinnacles, joined by giant mantas, jacks, snapper and the occasional sailfish or marlin.

dive_sites.name: Black Rock (Bajo Negro)
dive_sites.blurb: A steep pinnacle rising from around 30m toward the surface off Santa Rosa, drawing squadrons of eagle rays, dense schooling fish, occasional bull sharks and cruising manta rays.

dive_sites.name: Punta Gorda
dive_sites.blurb: A rocky point known for gliding groups of common and spotted eagle rays over the boulders, with turtles and reef fish in shallower water.

dive_sites.name: The Point
dive_sites.blurb: Current-swept ridges and boulder gardens holding schooling grunts, snapper and pompano, with rays and turtles working the edges.

dive_sites.name: Coral Gardens
dive_sites.blurb: A slightly more sheltered rocky-reef area used when conditions are rougher, with morays, invertebrates and reef fish and lighter current than the sharky pinnacles.

dive_sites.name: Bat Island Wall
dive_sites.blurb: A sloping wall along the islands where big pelagic schools, rays and passing bull sharks show up in the current, best dived on slack.

destinations.image_credit: Photo: Holobionics (CC BY-SA 4.0) via Wikimedia Commons
destinations.description: Bull sharks are the whole point of the Bat Islands (Islas Murcielago), a cluster of volcanic rocks off Costa Rica's Guanacaste coast reached by a committing 45km day-boat run from Playas del Coco. At the Big Scare, a dozen 2.5-3.5m bulls patrol pinnacles at 20-30m, with giant Pacific mantas, eagle-ray squadrons and walls of jacks for company. Strong current, surge and cool thermoclines make this advanced diving; bring a 5mm and your best buoyancy, June through October.

destinations.name: Coiba
destinations.access: Day-boat or multi-day trips from Santa Catalina (about 1 to 1.5 hours by boat to the nearest sites); remote northern Contreras Islands reached by liveaboard-style multi-night trips lodging at Pixvae or park islands.
destinations.wetsuit: 5mm full wetsuit (thermoclines can drop to 16-20C; rental centres often stock only 3mm, so bring your own)
destinations.current_note: Exposed seamounts and pinnacles run strong, sometimes shifting currents that draw pelagics; negative or semi-negative entries and blue-water ascents are common.
destinations.highlights: A Pacific UNESCO marine park where cool, nutrient-rich upwelling feeds schooling scalloped hammerheads (best March-July), resident whitetip reef sharks, seasonal whale sharks and mantas, plus walls of jacks and barracuda over coral-clad seamounts.

Jan.marine_life: Dry-season upwelling fuels big schools of jacks and barracuda, whitetip reef sharks and the start of whale shark chances
Jan.conditions: Calm seas, clear water, cool thermoclines below 15-20m

Feb.marine_life: Whale shark window continues, dense fish schools, turtles and mobulas over the seamounts
Feb.conditions: Dry, calm and clear; strong currents on exposed pinnacles

Mar.marine_life: Scalloped hammerhead schools build at pinnacles and Contreras; whale sharks still possible
Mar.conditions: Pronounced thermoclines begin bringing colder, plankton-rich water

Apr.marine_life: Prime hammerhead season with the biggest schools, plus whitetips, mantas and mobula rays
Apr.conditions: End of dry season, cool nutrient-rich currents, variable viz on thermoclines

May.marine_life: Hammerheads still schooling; abundant pelagic action as upwelling peaks
May.conditions: First rains arrive late month; strong currents and cool water

Jun.marine_life: Hammerheads best sought at the northern Contreras Islands; big-fish action holds
Jun.conditions: Rainy season underway, seas and visibility turn variable

Jul.marine_life: Hammerhead numbers taper; whitetip reef sharks, turtles and rays remain reliable
Jul.conditions: Wet and variable; run-off can cloud the water, third dives weather-dependent

Aug.marine_life: Resident reef life, whitetips and mobulas; passing mantas at plankton-rich sites
Aug.conditions: Peak rains, greener plankton-laden water, calmer seas between storms

Sep.marine_life: Reef sharks, turtles and macro life; fewer big schools as upwelling eases
Sep.conditions: Wettest stretch, reduced and inconsistent visibility

Oct.marine_life: Steady reef life and rays; pelagic action quietest of the year
Oct.conditions: Still rainy with murkier, plankton-rich water

Nov.marine_life: Fish schools and whitetips rebuild as conditions clear toward dry season
Nov.conditions: Rains ease late month; visibility improving in transition

Dec.marine_life: Dry-season return brings big schools, whitetips and the first whale shark sightings
Dec.conditions: Calming seas and clearing water; cool thermoclines at depth

dive_sites.name: Wahoo Rock
dive_sites.blurb: Coiba at its wildest: an offshore pinnacle of coral-, sponge- and fan-covered ridges swept by strong, shifting currents. Negative entries drop you into schooling jacks, barracuda, whitetips and the season's hammerheads.

dive_sites.name: Don Juan
dive_sites.blurb: A gentler rock-and-reef site of boulders and ledges hosting whitetip reef sharks resting on the sand, turtles, morays and dense reef fish, with occasional mobulas cruising the blue.

dive_sites.name: Frijoles
dive_sites.blurb: A colourful sloping reef good for wide-angle and macro alike: seahorses, frogfish, octopus and morays among the coral, with turtles and schooling snapper overhead.

dive_sites.name: Bajo Uno
dive_sites.blurb: A current-swept underwater pinnacle that concentrates pelagic life, from schooling hammerheads and big-fish tornadoes to passing mantas; entries and ascents are made in open blue water.

dive_sites.name: Buffet (La Viuda)
dive_sites.blurb: A signature Coiba pinnacle whose tip breaks the surface, wrapped in massive fish schools; plankton draws mantas and mobulas, while whitetips and turtles patrol the rocks.

dive_sites.name: Iglesia
dive_sites.blurb: Cathedral-like rock arches and swim-throughs give the site its name, sheltering reef fish, lobster and morays with pelagics passing along the outer walls.

dive_sites.name: Contreras Islands (Montana Rusa)
dive_sites.blurb: The park's far-north hotspot, reached only on multi-day trips: strong currents deliver the best odds at schooling hammerheads alongside whitetips, mobula rays and green turtles.

destinations.image_credit: Photo: Dronepicr (CC BY 3.0) via Wikimedia Commons
destinations.description: Panama's UNESCO-listed Coiba National Park protects a swath of eastern Pacific where cool, nutrient-rich upwelling turns seamounts into pelagic magnets: schooling scalloped hammerheads (strongest March through July), resident whitetip reef sharks, and seasonal whale sharks and mantas. Expect negative entries onto current-swept pinnacles like Wahoo Rock, thermoclines that can dip to 16-20C, and blue-water ascents. Most diving runs as day trips or multi-day expeditions out of Santa Catalina, and it firmly favors experienced divers.

destinations.name: Aqaba

Jan.marine_life: Quiet reefs, strong macro — seahorses, nudibranchs, octopus among resident anthias and lionfish
Jan.conditions: Cool, flat-calm gulf; crisp winter visibility, mild air

Feb.marine_life: Macro life plus occasional manta passing through the gulf
Feb.conditions: Coldest water of the year but sheltered and clear

Mar.marine_life: Reef fish activity picking up; turtles, frogfish and pipefish on the house reefs
Mar.conditions: Sea warming slowly, calm seas, reliably clear water

Apr.marine_life: Hawksbill turtles, eagle rays and schooling fusiliers over the coral gardens
Apr.conditions: Pleasant spring air, calm water, excellent visibility

May.marine_life: Plankton bloom draws bigger visitors — whale shark sightings possible late month
May.conditions: Warm and calm; bloom can soften visibility slightly

Jun.marine_life: Whale shark season peaks offshore; reefs busy with juveniles
Jun.conditions: Hot desert air, warm calm sea, long days

Jul.marine_life: Full summer reef activity — lionfish, moray eels, blue-spotted rays on the sand
Jul.conditions: Very hot topside; water at its warmest and glassy

Aug.marine_life: Warm-water reef life in full swing, octopus and cuttlefish at dusk
Aug.conditions: Peak heat on land, bath-warm flat sea below

Sep.marine_life: Abundant fish life — eagle rays, turtles and dense anthias clouds over the wrecks
Sep.conditions: Air cooling, sea still warm, visibility opening up

Oct.marine_life: Best all-round month: turtles, rays, thick reef fish and active macro
Oct.conditions: Ideal temperatures above and below, 30m-plus visibility

Nov.marine_life: Reefs still lively; seahorses and frogfish easier to find as crowds thin
Nov.conditions: Comfortable air, gently cooling water, superb clarity

Dec.marine_life: Resident reef life and macro; quiet, uncrowded sites
Dec.conditions: Cool clear water, calm sheltered gulf, mild winter days

destinations.highlights: Jordan's 27km Red Sea window packs the Cedar Pride wreck, a scuttled C-130 Hercules, a sunken tank and an underwater military museum alongside pristine coral gardens — nearly all divable straight off the beach.
destinations.currents: The Gulf of Aqaba is deeply sheltered, so currents are typically negligible to mild, with only a light longshore drift on some reef sites. This is one of the calmest stretches of the Red Sea and rarely dictates dive planning.
destinations.current_note: sheltered gulf, near-still water most days
destinations.wetsuit: 3mm full suit May-Nov; 5mm (some add a hood) Dec-Apr when water dips to 21°C

dive_sites.name: Cedar Pride
dive_sites.blurb: A soft-coral-carpeted Lebanese freighter lying on her port side 150m off the beach — one of the Red Sea's most accessible signature wrecks.

dive_sites.name: C-130 Hercules
dive_sites.blurb: An intact four-engine cargo plane scuttled in 2017, sitting upright on sand with an easy cockpit-to-tail swim-through.

dive_sites.name: The Tank
dive_sites.blurb: An M42 anti-aircraft tank sunk just 20m from shore, now crusted in coral and a favourite for training dives and long safety-stop photo ops.

dive_sites.name: Japanese Garden
dive_sites.blurb: Aqaba's prettiest coral slope — table, brain and soft corals in botanical-garden density with turtles cruising the shallows.

dive_sites.name: Underwater Military Museum
dive_sites.blurb: A battle-formation column of scuttled tanks, troop carriers and a helicopter arranged down the reef slope since 2019.

dive_sites.name: Power Station
dive_sites.blurb: A dramatic drop-off at the northern end of the coast with black coral, gorgonians and the best chance of pelagic fly-bys.

destinations.description: Jordan's single short coastline squeezes remarkable variety into the calm, current-free northern tip of the Red Sea: a legendary freighter wreck, a scuttled cargo plane, sunken military hardware and genuinely healthy fringing reefs, almost all reached by walking in from the beach. Water stays warm and flat year-round, visibility rarely drops below 20m, and macro hunting is superb. Ideal for new divers, wreck lovers and anyone pairing dives with Petra and Wadi Rum.
destinations.image_credit: Photo: Tiia Monto (CC BY-SA 3.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Aruba

Jan.marine_life: Green and hawksbill turtles on the reefs, barracuda over the wrecks, stingrays on sand
Jan.conditions: Dry and sunny with steady 20-25 mph trades; protected west coast stays diveable

Feb.marine_life: Turtles, southern stingrays and seahorses in the seagrass; reef fish thick on the Antilla
Feb.conditions: Driest, coolest water of the year; brisk trade winds, calm leeward sites

Mar.marine_life: Eagle rays cruising outer reefs, turtles everywhere, wrecks carpeted in tube sponges and fish
Mar.conditions: Peak dry season; calm, clear water on the sheltered west coast

Apr.marine_life: Eagle rays and stingrays active, turtle sightings building toward nesting season
Apr.conditions: Sunny and settled with excellent clarity; ideal wreck-diving window

May.marine_life: Turtle nesting season begins; frequent green and hawksbill encounters, barracuda schools
May.conditions: Warm and dry; trades strengthen but leeward sites remain flat

Jun.marine_life: Turtles at their most abundant, rays over the sand flats, dense fish on the Jane Sea
Jun.conditions: Windiest stretch of the year; surface chop, though west-coast dives stay easy

Jul.marine_life: Turtle hatchlings and adults, frogfish and seahorses for sharp-eyed divers
Jul.conditions: Strong trades continue; reliable sun, warm water, mild surface texture

Aug.marine_life: Late-season turtles, eagle rays on the outer reef, octopus on night dives
Aug.conditions: Hot and breezy; well outside the hurricane belt so seas stay predictable

Sep.marine_life: Warmest water draws big barracuda and rays; juvenile fish swarm the wrecks
Sep.conditions: Trades ease to their calmest; glassy mornings and long viz

Oct.marine_life: Rays, turtles and resident wreck life unchanged; macro critters in the seagrass
Oct.conditions: Warmest sea of the year; brief showers begin, occasional fringe swell

Nov.marine_life: Turtles and stingrays year-round; reef fish activity steady on the leeward reefs
Nov.conditions: Wettest month, though rain falls in short bursts; slightly softer viz

Dec.marine_life: Turtles, barracuda and eagle rays back on the usual circuits
Dec.conditions: Showers taper, trades rebuild; busy holiday season with easy conditions

destinations.highlights: The 120m Antilla — one of the Caribbean's largest diveable wrecks — headlines a leeward coast of WWII ships, sunken airplanes and shore-accessible reefs that dive well twelve months a year outside the hurricane belt.
destinations.currents: Constant northeast trade winds drive a west-flowing current along the island, so diving concentrates on the sheltered leeward coast where it is usually mild. Southern sites like the Jane Sea and Baby Beach Reef can run strong and are dived as drifts when it picks up.
destinations.current_note: mild on the leeward wrecks; can run strong at the southern reef sites
destinations.wetsuit: 3mm shorty or full suit year-round; many divers go rash-guard only Aug-Oct

dive_sites.name: SS Antilla
dive_sites.blurb: A 120m German freighter scuttled in 1940 and one of the Caribbean's largest wrecks, shallow enough to cover in a single dive and blanketed in tube sponges and fish.

dive_sites.name: Pedernales
dive_sites.blurb: The scattered midsection of an oil tanker torpedoed by a U-boat in 1942, lying in calm shallows with cabins and pipeline sections to weave through.

dive_sites.name: Jane Sea
dive_sites.blurb: An upright 52m cement freighter sunk as an artificial reef in 1988, thickly overgrown with coral and swept by the currents that bring in barracuda and rays.

dive_sites.name: Sonesta Airplanes
dive_sites.blurb: A YS-11 turboprop and a deeper DC-3 sunk off Renaissance Island, giving Aruba its signature airplane-wreck double bill.

dive_sites.name: Mangel Halto
dive_sites.blurb: The island's best shore dive: a mangrove-fringed lagoon spilling onto a sloping reef where turtles are near-guaranteed and seahorses hide in the grass.

dive_sites.name: Baby Beach Reef
dive_sites.blurb: A hard-coral reef at the island's southeastern tip known for barracuda and turtles, with current that rewards a watchful drift.

destinations.description: Aruba sits at the Caribbean's southern edge, below the hurricane belt, where steady trade winds shape a dry, sunny island and a sheltered leeward coast that dives reliably all year. Wrecks are the story here: the vast Antilla, the torpedoed Pedernales, a coral-covered freighter and a pair of sunken airplanes, most in easy recreational depths. Warm water, short boat rides and genuine shore entries make it a natural pick for new wreck divers and mixed-ability groups.
destinations.image_credit: Photo: Serge Melki from Indianapolis, USA (CC BY 2.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Bali

Jan.marine_life: resident wreck fish, nudibranch numbers climbing on the muck slopes, bumpheads at dawn on the Liberty
Jan.conditions: wet-season rains and runoff soften visibility; east-coast shore entries stay sheltered and diveable

Feb.marine_life: macro life thick at Seraya and the Padang Bai jetty; frogfish, ghost pipefish and octopus on the black sand
Feb.conditions: rainiest stretch of the year; mornings calmest, viz variable after downpours

Mar.marine_life: reef fish spawning activity, turtles on the walls, harlequin shrimp and boxer crabs in the muck
Mar.conditions: rains easing, seas settling; visibility recovering on the wreck and at Menjangan

Apr.marine_life: schooling jacks and sweetlips crowd the Liberty; pygmy seahorses on the fans, reef sharks off the drop-offs
Apr.conditions: dry season begins — flat seas, warm water and fast-improving clarity coast-wide

May.marine_life: wreck at its liveliest — bumphead parrotfish at first light, garden eels, dense fish soup over the coral
May.conditions: glassy mornings and superb visibility; prime month for the east coast

Jun.marine_life: big schools on the wreck and walls, turtles at Menjangan, full macro spread at Amed and Seraya
Jun.conditions: dry, calm and clear across all four areas; light southeast trades

Jul.marine_life: cooler water wakes up the critters — rhinopias and blue-ringed octopus sightings pick up at Padang Bai
Jul.conditions: dry and clear; trade winds add chop to boat crossings, water noticeably cooler

Aug.marine_life: reef sharks and schooling fish thrive in the cool upwellings; nudibranchs everywhere on the muck slopes
Aug.conditions: coolest water and briskest winds of the year; viz strong but some swell on exposed sites

Sep.marine_life: fish life dense on the Liberty, thermocline critters still active, turtles steady at Menjangan
Sep.conditions: dry and reliable; winds beginning to ease, water slowly warming

Oct.marine_life: peak fish activity on the wreck and walls; gorgonian fans at Menjangan hosting pygmy seahorses
Oct.conditions: calm seas and the year's clearest water, especially over Menjangan's walls

Nov.marine_life: nudibranch numbers surge as plankton returns; wreck and Blue Lagoon fishy and photogenic
Nov.conditions: clarity still exceptional early in the month before the first rains arrive

Dec.marine_life: resident wreck fauna, mandarinfish and frogfish at the jetty, macro season ramping up
Dec.conditions: wet season building — warm, sheltered shore diving with softer visibility

destinations.highlights: A coral-encrusted WWII wreck you can walk into off a pebble beach, world-class black-sand muck at Seraya and Padang Bai, and Menjangan's sheer gorgonian walls — all shore- or short-boat accessible year-round.
destinations.currents: The northeast coast at Tulamben and Amed is sheltered, with gentle currents that rarely trouble shore entries. Sites in the Padang Bai channel feel the Lombok Strait's pull and can switch on quickly, so guides time slack windows there.
destinations.current_note: calm shore sites; channel sites near Padang Bai can run
destinations.wetsuit: 3mm full year-round; 5mm for the cooler Jul-Sep water and repetitive days

dive_sites.name: USAT Liberty, Tulamben
dive_sites.blurb: A 120m WWII cargo ship smothered in coral just 30m off the beach — arguably the world's easiest great wreck dive, with bumphead parrotfish at dawn.

dive_sites.name: Tulamben Drop-Off
dive_sites.blurb: A river-mouth wall plunging past big gorgonians and barrel sponges, with reef sharks and schooling fish in the blue.

dive_sites.name: Seraya Secrets
dive_sites.blurb: Bali's most famous black-sand macro slope, where photographers hunt harlequin shrimp, boxer crabs and ghost pipefish.

dive_sites.name: Blue Lagoon, Padang Bai
dive_sites.blurb: A calm white-sand bay packed with unusual critters — rhinopias, leaf scorpionfish and blue-ringed octopus turn up here.

dive_sites.name: Jemeluk Wall & Pyramids, Amed
dive_sites.blurb: An easy shore-entry wall and artificial-reef pyramids alive with glassfish, garden eels and passing turtles.

dive_sites.name: Menjangan Island
dive_sites.blurb: Sheer walls in a marine park off Bali's northwest tip, famous for the island's clearest water and gorgonian-draped drop-offs.

destinations.description: Bali's mainland coast packs remarkable variety into short taxi rides: the coral-carpeted USAT Liberty wreck at Tulamben, black-sand muck slopes at Seraya and Amed, critter-rich bays at Padang Bai, and Menjangan's crystalline walls in the northwest. Warm, mostly gentle water and shore entries make it a superb place to build skills, while the macro life keeps seasoned photographers busy for weeks. Dry-season months bring the flattest seas and clearest water.
destinations.image_credit: Photo: (c) Bernard DUPONT, some rights reserved (CC BY-SA) (CC BY-SA 4.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Cancún & Playa del Carmen

Jan.marine_life: Bull sharks at their thickest off Playa; sailfish balling baitfish offshore; eagle rays on the wrecks
Jan.conditions: Dry season, coolest water, superb clarity; odd norte blows through for a day or two

Feb.marine_life: Pregnant bull sharks still stacked at Shark Point; sailfish run in full swing; turtles on every drift
Feb.conditions: Dry, settled seas between fronts, excellent visibility

Mar.marine_life: Last of the bull sharks through mid-month; sailfish action offshore; reef fish and rays everywhere
Mar.conditions: Dry and warming, calm seas, very good visibility

Apr.marine_life: Turtles, eagle rays and nurse sharks on the reefs; big schools over the sculptures at MUSA
Apr.conditions: Flat, warm and clear; one of the calmest, least crowded windows

May.marine_life: First whale sharks arrive on the offshore banks mid-month; turtles nesting season begins
May.conditions: Warm and mostly calm; sargassum starts drifting onto beaches but reefs stay clean

Jun.marine_life: Whale shark numbers building offshore; reef life prolific, turtles abundant
Jun.conditions: Hot, rainy season starts; short afternoon storms, sea still diveable most days

Jul.marine_life: Hundreds of whale sharks aggregate offshore — the world's largest gathering; turtles and rays inshore
Jul.conditions: Hot and humid, bathtub-warm water, generally calm mornings

Aug.marine_life: Whale shark aggregation at its height; mantas sometimes mixed in; turtles on the reefs
Aug.conditions: Warmest water of the year; calm spells broken by tropical squalls

Sep.marine_life: Whale sharks disperse mid-month; reef standards — turtles, morays, barracuda — carry the diving
Sep.conditions: Peak hurricane month; diveable between systems but cancellation risk is real

Oct.marine_life: Quiet pelagic month; healthy reef life, rays and turtles on the drifts
Oct.conditions: Tail of hurricane season, rain easing late in the month, visibility recovering

Nov.marine_life: First bull sharks appear off Playa mid-month; eagle rays returning to the wrecks
Nov.conditions: Dry season begins; first nortes can stir the sea for a day or two

Dec.marine_life: Bull shark season in full swing at 24m off Playa; sailfish arriving offshore; eagle rays schooling
Dec.conditions: Cooling, clear dry-season water; occasional norte, otherwise excellent

destinations.highlights: Two headline shark seasons — cageless bull shark dives at 24m off Playa del Carmen from November to March and the planet's biggest whale shark aggregation offshore in July-August — bolted onto easy drift reefs, hurricane-split wrecks and the sculpture galleries of the MUSA underwater museum on Manchones reef.
destinations.currents: The north-flowing Yucatán Current sweeps the coast, so most Playa del Carmen reef diving is done as relaxed one-way drifts with the boat following. Inshore Cancún sites like Manchones and MUSA sit in protected shallows with little water movement, while the deeper wrecks can carry genuinely strong flow.
destinations.current_note: Drift-style boat diving on the Playa reefs; strong current possible on the deep wrecks
destinations.wetsuit: 3mm full year-round; 5mm comfortable for repetitive dives Dec-Mar

dive_sites.name: MUSA — Manchones
dive_sites.blurb: The underwater museum's main gallery, where hundreds of life-size sculptures on Manchones reef have become coral-crusted habitat in calm, clear shallows.

dive_sites.name: Manchones Reef
dive_sites.blurb: Long, shallow ridge of hard coral off Isla Mujeres with big schools of grunts and snapper, gentle conditions and generous bottom time.

dive_sites.name: Shark Point
dive_sites.blurb: A featureless sand flat off Playa del Carmen where kneeling divers watch pregnant bull sharks cruise within metres from November to March.

dive_sites.name: C-58 General Anaya
dive_sites.blurb: WWII-era minesweeper broken in two by hurricanes, opening swim-throughs that fill with eagle rays in winter; a live-drop drift dive in current.

dive_sites.name: Mama Viña
dive_sites.blurb: Shrimp trawler sunk as an artificial reef off Playa, sitting upright on sand and swept by current that keeps it dense with barracuda and sponges.

dive_sites.name: Tortugas
dive_sites.blurb: Playa's turtle alley — a mellow drift over sponge-covered slopes where green and loggerhead turtles feed at close range.

dive_sites.name: Los Arcos
dive_sites.blurb: Deeper Playa reef of arches, overhangs and swim-throughs that pulls in nurse sharks, rays and the occasional passing pelagic.

destinations.description: The Cancún–Playa del Carmen corridor is high-volume Caribbean diving with two genuinely wild seasons stitched in. Winter brings pregnant bull sharks to a 24-metre sand flat off Playa and sailfish to the blue; summer stacks hundreds of whale sharks offshore. Between them you drift warm, easy reefs on the Yucatán Current, land on hurricane-split wrecks, and tour the sculpture galleries of MUSA. Short boat rides and year-round warm water suit everyone from new divers to shark-focused veterans.

destinations.name: Dubai & Fujairah
destinations.region: Gulf of Oman / United Arab Emirates

Jan.marine_life: Turtles and blacktip reef sharks on Dibba Rock, cuttlefish pairing up, big morays on the wrecks
Jan.conditions: Cool, calm winter seas; the year's clearest water

Feb.marine_life: Cuttlefish courtship, eagle rays, jawfish colonies in the sand, resident turtles
Feb.conditions: Coolest water of the year but settled and clear

Mar.marine_life: Rays and guitarfish over the sand, nudibranchs blooming on Martini Rock, turtles everywhere
Mar.conditions: Warming, mostly calm; occasional plankton haze starts

Apr.marine_life: Baitballs building, barracuda schools, blacktips patrolling the shallows
Apr.conditions: Warm and pleasant; spring blooms can soften the viz

May.marine_life: First whale shark reports around Dibba Rock, snapper aggregations, batfish on the Inchcapes
May.conditions: Getting hot topside; sea still friendly, viz variable

Jun.marine_life: Whale shark season begins in earnest, plankton feeders active, turtles unbothered by the heat
Jun.conditions: Bathwater sea, hazy plankton-rich water, hot boat rides

Jul.marine_life: Best whale shark odds of the year, big schools of fusiliers and jacks in the green water
Jul.conditions: Sea over 30°C, milky visibility, extreme surface heat

Aug.marine_life: Whale sharks still possible; reef life subdued in the warmest, greenest water
Aug.conditions: Peak heat; algal blooms most likely and viz can collapse

Sep.marine_life: Late whale sharks, barracuda tornadoes returning, morays and scorpionfish thick on the wrecks
Sep.conditions: Still hot; bloom risk lingers but the sea starts to settle

Oct.marine_life: Rays back over the sand, schooling snapper and trevally, turtles on every Snoopy Island dive
Oct.conditions: Cooling and clearing; comfortable diving resumes

Nov.marine_life: Blacktips, honeycomb morays, cuttlefish arriving, dense fish life on Martini Rock
Nov.conditions: Calm, clear and comfortable — the sweet spot begins

Dec.marine_life: Turtles, eagle rays, cuttlefish and prolific macro on the coral outcrops
Dec.conditions: Cool, glassy winter conditions with the clearest water

destinations.highlights: Easy year-round diving on the Gulf of Oman east coast — turtle-packed reefs at Dibba Rock and Snoopy Island, soft-coral-crusted Martini Rock, the purpose-sunk Inchcape wrecks, plus Dubai's artificial-reef wreck sites, with summer whale shark visits.
destinations.currents: Currents on the east coast are generally mild, with only occasional gentle drift along exposed outcrops like Martini Rock and the Inchcape wrecks. Surge can pick up in the shallows when swell wraps around Dibba Rock.
destinations.current_note: mostly slack; light current or surge on exposed sites
destinations.wetsuit: 3mm full Oct-May (5mm midwinter for the cold-prone); shorty or 1mm skin in 31-32°C summer water

dive_sites.name: Dibba Rock
dive_sites.blurb: A protected rocky islet whose sloping reef delivers turtles on nearly every dive, patrolling blacktips and the coast's most reliable whale shark encounters in summer.

dive_sites.name: Snoopy Island
dive_sites.blurb: A shore-accessible boulder islet off Al Aqah where hard-coral gardens shelter turtles, rays and dense schooling fish in easy, shallow water.

dive_sites.name: Martini Rock
dive_sites.blurb: A submerged pinnacle carpeted in orange teddy-bear soft coral, famous for macro life, nudibranchs, morays and winter cuttlefish.

dive_sites.name: Inchcape 1
dive_sites.blurb: A 21m supply vessel purpose-sunk in 2001, sitting upright at 30m and now smothered in growth with lionfish, jacks and big morays.

dive_sites.name: Inchcape 2
dive_sites.blurb: The shallower sister wreck near Martini Wall, sunk in 2002 as an accessible artificial reef busy with batfish, snapper and scorpionfish.

dive_sites.name: Car Cemetery
dive_sites.blurb: Rows of sunken vehicles on the Fujairah sand that have grown into a quirky artificial reef stacked with morays, rays and glassfish.

dive_sites.name: Zainab Wreck
dive_sites.blurb: Dubai's signature wreck — a 70m smuggling tanker scuttled in 2001, lying on her port side and swarmed by barracuda, snapper and batfish.

destinations.description: The UAE's real diving lives on the Fujairah side, where the Gulf of Oman's plankton-rich water feeds rocky reefs at Dibba Rock, Snoopy Island and Martini Rock, plus the purpose-sunk Inchcape wrecks; Dubai adds sandy-bottom wreck sites and artificial reefs in the Gulf. Expect warm, easy conditions with modest visibility that peaks in winter and can green out during summer blooms — when whale sharks arrive. Ideal for new divers, wreck fans and layover diving.
destinations.image_credit: Photo: Imre Solt (CC BY-SA 3.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Florida Keys

Jan.marine_life: Reef fish, turtles, nurse sharks; sailfish and bait balls offshore
Jan.conditions: Cold fronts bring wind and chop; superb clarity between fronts

Feb.marine_life: Grunts and snapper schooling thick; spotted eagle rays cruising the reef edge
Feb.conditions: Coolest water of the year; front-driven blowouts possible, calm days crystal clear

Mar.marine_life: Reef life waking up; eagle rays, turtles, big barracuda on the wrecks
Mar.conditions: Fronts taper off, seas settling; water still cool

Apr.marine_life: Turtles nesting season begins; reef fish spawning activity picks up
Apr.conditions: Calming seas, warming water, reliable boat schedules

May.marine_life: Full reef activity — angelfish, parrotfish, Caribbean reef sharks on the deep wrecks
May.conditions: Flat calm stretches, warm water, long daylight

Jun.marine_life: Tarpon and permit on the wrecks; nurse sharks and turtles everywhere
Jun.conditions: Warm and mostly calm; hurricane season starts but early risk is low

Jul.marine_life: Peak reef abundance; lobster walking the reef, big jacks over the wrecks
Jul.conditions: Bathwater-warm, glassy mornings; busiest boats of the year

Aug.marine_life: Goliath grouper begin aggregating on the deep wrecks; coral spawning after the full moon
Aug.conditions: Hot and calm between systems; watch the tropics for storms

Sep.marine_life: Goliath grouper aggregation peaks — dozens of 200kg fish on Spiegel Grove and the Vandenberg
Sep.conditions: Statistical hurricane peak; superb diving when the weather window holds

Oct.marine_life: Lingering goliaths, thick baitfish clouds, reef sharks patrolling
Oct.conditions: Storm risk fades late month; warm water, thinning crowds

Nov.marine_life: Reef fish dense, eagle rays returning; wrecks quiet and fishy
Nov.conditions: First mild fronts arrive; mostly diveable with clearing visibility

Dec.marine_life: Turtles, nurse sharks and schooling grunts; occasional offshore pelagics
Dec.conditions: Cold fronts return with windy spells; clear, cooler water between them

destinations.highlights: America's reef tract in one island chain — the towering Spiegel Grove and Vandenberg wrecks, the Christ of the Abyss statue, spur-and-groove gardens at Molasses Reef and Looe Key, and a late-summer goliath grouper aggregation that turns the deep wrecks into cathedrals of giants.
destinations.currents: The Gulf Stream runs just offshore, washing the outer reefs with clear water and pushing moderate — occasionally strong — current across the deep wrecks like the Duane and Spiegel Grove. Inshore spur-and-groove reefs are usually gentle.
destinations.current_note: Deep wrecks can rip when the Gulf Stream swings in; descend on the mooring line
destinations.wetsuit: Shorty or 3mm in summer; 5mm full suit December-March

dive_sites.name: Spiegel Grove
dive_sites.blurb: A 155m landing ship dock off Key Largo — one of the world's largest artificial reefs, sprawling enough for a dozen dives and a goliath grouper magnet in late summer.

dive_sites.name: Christ of the Abyss
dive_sites.blurb: A bronze statue standing arms-raised in the shallows of Key Largo Dry Rocks, ringed by coral heads and clouds of grunts — the Keys' most photographed dive.

dive_sites.name: Molasses Reef
dive_sites.blurb: Gulf Stream-washed spur-and-groove with the best visibility in the Upper Keys, huge snapper schools and the old Slobodna windlass.

dive_sites.name: USNS Vandenberg
dive_sites.blurb: A 159m missile-tracking ship off Key West whose giant radar dishes make it one of the most photogenic wrecks in the Atlantic.

dive_sites.name: Looe Key
dive_sites.blurb: The Lower Keys' classic spur-and-groove sanctuary reef, dense with elkhorn relics, parrotfish and resident goliath grouper.

dive_sites.name: USCGC Duane
dive_sites.blurb: An upright, fully intact coast guard cutter sitting in the Gulf Stream's path — current-swept, sharky and blue.

destinations.description: The Florida Keys string 200km of the continental US's only coral barrier reef alongside a fleet of massive purpose-sunk warships. Days here alternate easy spur-and-groove reef dives at Molasses and Looe Key with serious deep wrecks — Spiegel Grove, Duane, Vandenberg — and in August and September goliath grouper aggregate on those hulls by the dozen. Easy logistics and warm water suit new divers; the wrecks reward advanced tickets. Hurricane season demands flexible plans.
destinations.image_credit: Photo: Tane Casserley/National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (Public domain) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Koh Tao

Jan.marine_life: Reef fish rebounding after monsoon; barracuda schools returning to the pinnacles
Jan.conditions: Northeast monsoon easing; seas calming, visibility slowly recovering

Feb.marine_life: Chevron barracuda and trevally back in numbers; healthy macro in the bays
Feb.conditions: Often glassy, mirror-calm seas; clear water and light winds

Mar.marine_life: Whale shark season begins as plankton blooms draw them to Chumphon Pinnacle
Mar.conditions: Hot, dry and flat calm; superb visibility on the offshore pinnacles

Apr.marine_life: Best odds of whale sharks; giant trevally hunting fusilier balls on the pinnacles
Apr.conditions: Hottest, calmest stretch of the year; visibility at its best

May.marine_life: Whale sharks still passing through; big schools of batfish and barracuda
May.conditions: Warm and mostly calm; first southwest-monsoon breezes late in the month

Jun.marine_life: Resident pinnacle life steady; groupers, snapper and triggerfish nesting in the shallows
Jun.conditions: Southwest monsoon brings occasional westerly chop; east-coast sites stay sheltered

Jul.marine_life: Fusilier and barracuda schools thick at Chumphon and Southwest Pinnacle
Jul.conditions: Short showers and some wind-driven waves; visibility variable but often fine

Aug.marine_life: Trevally hunts and dense baitfish; occasional whale shark surprise
Aug.conditions: Often surprisingly calm and clear despite the season

Sep.marine_life: Second whale-shark window; pinnacles at their fishiest
Sep.conditions: Calm seas and clear water before the northeast monsoon builds

Oct.marine_life: Whale sharks still possible early; schooling fish remain thick
Oct.conditions: Transition month; increasing showers and the first monsoon swells

Nov.marine_life: Reef life sheltering; sightings limited by runoff-clouded water
Nov.conditions: Northeast monsoon peaks with heavy rain, wind and the year's worst visibility

Dec.marine_life: Hardy reef residents only; big animals scarce in stirred-up water
Dec.conditions: Strong winds and frequent downpours; boats weather-dependent, viz reduced

destinations.highlights: Granite pinnacles rising from the Gulf of Thailand where whale sharks cruise through walls of barracuda, ringed by calm, shallow bays that make some of the easiest learning conditions anywhere.
destinations.currents: The sheltered bays around the island are nearly current-free, which is why so many divers learn here. The offshore pinnacles — Chumphon, Southwest and Sail Rock — can carry moderate current that concentrates the schooling fish.
destinations.current_note: calm bays; moderate flow possible on the offshore pinnacles
destinations.wetsuit: 3mm shorty or rash guard year-round; 3mm full if you run cold in Dec-Feb

dive_sites.name: Chumphon Pinnacle
dive_sites.blurb: The Gulf's marquee site — an anemone-carpeted granite tower swarmed by trevally and barracuda, and the island's most reliable whale-shark spot.

dive_sites.name: Sail Rock
dive_sites.blurb: A lone rock between Koh Tao and Koh Phangan with a vertical chimney swim-through and frequent whale-shark visits.

dive_sites.name: Southwest Pinnacle
dive_sites.blurb: A cluster of anemone-topped peaks patrolled by big groupers and dense barracuda schools.

dive_sites.name: HTMS Sattakut
dive_sites.blurb: A 48m former navy vessel sunk as an artificial reef, its gun turrets now home to groupers and glassfish.

dive_sites.name: White Rock
dive_sites.blurb: A sprawling two-part reef busy with angelfish and territorial titan triggerfish — the island's classic night dive.

dive_sites.name: Twins
dive_sites.blurb: Two boulder clusters in easy shallows, famous for a resident family of saddleback anemonefish.

destinations.description: Koh Tao is where the Gulf of Thailand earns its reputation as an easy, generous place to dive: warm, calm bays over shallow reef for new divers, and offshore granite pinnacles that punch far above their weight. Chumphon Pinnacle and Sail Rock stack barracuda, trevally and — in spring and early autumn — whale sharks over anemone-covered rock. Budget-friendly and boat rides are short; skip the November-December monsoon and it delivers year-round.
destinations.image_credit: Photo: User: (WT-shared) Alf Birnecker at  wts wikivoyage (CC BY-SA 1.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Mallorca

Jan.marine_life: resident grouper, octopus, moray and scorpionfish on quiet reefs
Jan.conditions: cold water, winter swells, many operators closed; viz often surprisingly clear

Feb.marine_life: grouper, conger and lobster deep in the caves; nudibranchs start appearing
Feb.conditions: coldest water of the year, weather-dependent boat days

Mar.marine_life: nudibranchs peak, octopus active, first cuttlefish inshore
Mar.conditions: still cold, mixed seas as winter fronts fade

Apr.marine_life: cuttlefish and octopus mating inshore, grouper emerging from winter lies
Apr.conditions: seas settle, water slowly warming, dive centres reopening

May.marine_life: barracuda schools regroup over El Toro, dusky grouper bold in the reserves
May.conditions: calm spells lengthen, water warming, clear pre-season viz

Jun.marine_life: big barracuda tornadoes, grouper, eagle rays passing, cave life everywhere
Jun.conditions: warm, flat summer seas begin; long daylight, excellent viz

Jul.marine_life: dense barracuda and bream schools, tame dusky grouper, octopus and moray on every dive
Jul.conditions: warmest, calmest seas of the year; light beams in the caverns

Aug.marine_life: barracuda walls at El Toro and Malgrats, grouper, occasional eagle ray and amberjack
Aug.conditions: peak water warmth and 30m-plus viz, but busiest boats and sites

Sep.marine_life: marine life at its richest — hunting amberjack, barracuda, grouper, rays
Sep.conditions: sea still warm, crowds thin, generally settled with first autumn storms late

Oct.marine_life: barracuda and grouper still thick in the reserves, cuttlefish returning
Oct.conditions: pleasantly warm water, quieter sites, occasional storm days

Nov.marine_life: reef life settling into winter mode; grouper, moray, lobster in the overhangs
Nov.conditions: cooling fast, more wind and swell, many centres winding down

Dec.marine_life: hardy residents — grouper, conger, octopus — for those who brave it
Dec.conditions: cold water and winter weather windows; limited operator schedules

destinations.highlights: Sunlit Mediterranean caverns, swim-throughs and air-dome caves paired with the barracuda schools and tame dusky grouper of the El Toro and Malgrats marine reserves.
destinations.currents: The Balearics are largely tide-free, so most sites see little to no current. Exposed headlands and the offshore reserve islets can pick up mild surface current or surge when the wind has been blowing.
destinations.current_note: mostly still water; light surge at exposed islets
destinations.wetsuit: 3-5mm full suit Jun-Oct; 7mm semi-dry or drysuit Nov-May

dive_sites.name: El Toro Marine Reserve
dive_sites.blurb: Protected islet where dense barracuda schools and fearless dusky grouper patrol a sloping reef that drops past 35m for conger and john dory.

dive_sites.name: Malgrats Islands
dive_sites.blurb: Twin-islet marine reserve whose wall, tunnels and arches hold midwater barracuda, big bream, grouper and lobster.

dive_sites.name: The Cathedral
dive_sites.blurb: Vast cavern with a breathable air dome, sheltering moray, scorpionfish, crawfish and octopus in its shadows.

dive_sites.name: El Formatge (The Cheese)
dive_sites.blurb: Hole-riddled rock islet off Cala Ratjada laced with swim-through tunnels frequented by octopus, moray and nudibranchs.

dive_sites.name: Sa Dragonera
dive_sites.blurb: Nature-park island off the west coast with clear-water walls, caves and passing pelagics.

dive_sites.name: Cave of Light (Portals Vells)
dive_sites.blurb: Shallow cavern famous for the shafts of sunlight that cut through its blue entrance on calm summer days.

destinations.description: Mallorca is the Mediterranean's limestone playground: decades of erosion have drilled its coastline into caverns, arches and swim-throughs, most of them shallow, sunlit and forgiving. The El Toro and Malgrats marine reserves supply the fish — wall-to-wall barracuda and dusky grouper so protected they ignore you. With tide-free seas, easy day-boat logistics and 30-metre summer visibility, it suits new divers building skills and cavern fans alike; May to October is the season that matters.
destinations.image_credit: Photo: Strubbl (CC BY-SA 4.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Mauritius
destinations.difficulty: beginner-friendly, with advanced wrecks and caverns

Jan.marine_life: Warm-water reef life at its busiest; turtles, moray eels and reef sharks on the west coast
Jan.conditions: Hot and humid with cyclone risk building; calm spells give lovely diving between systems

Feb.marine_life: Reef fish and turtles still about, but swell and runoff can scatter the action
Feb.conditions: Peak cyclone month; storms can blow out diving for days and stir the lagoons

Mar.marine_life: Warmest water of the year keeps reefs lively; frogfish and morays on the wrecks
Mar.conditions: Cyclone risk tapering but still real; settled weeks dive very well

Apr.marine_life: Full tropical spread — turtles, barracuda, grey reef sharks at the Cathedral drop-off
Apr.conditions: Cyclone season over, winds light and seas settled; some of the year's clearest water

May.marine_life: Big schools build as water cools; dogtooth tuna and pelagics start cruising the north
May.conditions: Calm, dry early-winter weather; comfortable seas on the sheltered west coast

Jun.marine_life: Pelagic season — tuna, trevally and dense fish shoals; first humpback whales arrive offshore
Jun.conditions: Cooler and breezier as trade winds pick up; west-coast sites stay protected

Jul.marine_life: Humpback whales migrating past; shoaling fish, spinner dolphins and macro critters on the reefs
Jul.conditions: Coolest water and steady southeast trades; morning dives on the west coast are calmest

Aug.marine_life: Humpback activity peaks offshore; reef life steady with morays, scorpionfish and nudibranchs
Aug.conditions: Windiest month with swell on exposed coasts; sheltered west-coast diving only some days

Sep.marine_life: Whales still passing; big schools linger and turtles are reliable on the wrecks
Sep.conditions: Winds beginning to ease; cool, clear water and improving sea states

Oct.marine_life: Warming water wakes the reefs; sharks at Coin de Mire, turtles and dense wreck life
Oct.conditions: Dry, settled shoulder season — light winds, flat seas and excellent visibility

Nov.marine_life: High season for diversity — reef sharks, barracuda, rays and thick reef-fish aggregations
Nov.conditions: Warm, calm and clear before the wet season; arguably the year's best all-round conditions

Dec.marine_life: Summer abundance in full swing; turtles, morays and pelagics around the northern islets
Dec.conditions: Warm and mostly settled early in the month; humidity and shower risk rise toward New Year

destinations.highlights: A volcanic island ringed by lagoon and drop-off, pairing the cavern-lit Cathedral at Flic en Flac with fish-thick wrecks like the Stella Maru and Djabeda and shark-patrolled walls off Coin de Mire islet.
destinations.currents: Most lagoon-side and west-coast sites are calm, but exposed reefs like Rempart Serpent and the passes around Coin de Mire can run moderate to strong with the tide. Boats time slack water on the wrecks, so currents rarely dictate the day.
destinations.current_note: gentle inside the reef, brisker on exposed northern passes
destinations.wetsuit: 3mm full in summer (Nov-Apr); 5mm in winter (Jun-Sep) when water dips to 23°C

dive_sites.name: Cathedral
dive_sites.blurb: Flic en Flac's signature dive — a shaft of light pours into a boulder-roofed chamber on the drop-off where grey reef sharks and soldierfish shelter.

dive_sites.name: Stella Maru
dive_sites.blurb: A trawler scuttled off Trou aux Biches decades ago, now a coral-dressed artificial reef with frogfish, porcupinefish and a resident giant moray.

dive_sites.name: Djabeda
dive_sites.blurb: A 44m Japanese fishing vessel sitting upright off Coin de Mire since 1998, wrapped in soft coral and circled by tuna and honeycomb morays.

dive_sites.name: The Wall, Coin de Mire
dive_sites.blurb: A sheer face off the northern islet where clear oceanic water brings sharks, barracuda and passing pelagics close to the rock.

dive_sites.name: Rempart Serpent
dive_sites.blurb: A snake-shaped ridge off Flic en Flac famous for an improbable density of scorpionfish, stonefish and moray eels on one small reef.

dive_sites.name: Blue Bay Marine Park
dive_sites.blurb: A protected southeastern lagoon and outer reef with healthy hard coral gardens — the island's gentlest introduction to its underwater life.

destinations.description: Mauritius wraps a volcanic island in barrier reef, so most diving happens minutes offshore where the lagoon wall drops into blue water. The west coast around Flic en Flac delivers the light-shafted Cathedral cavern and macro-rich ridges, while the north stacks wrecks and shark walls around Coin de Mire islet. It suits divers who want variety — caverns, scuttled ships, coral gardens — without long crossings, though January-to-March cyclones can interrupt; October to December and April to May dive best.
destinations.image_credit: Photo: "dronepicr" (CC BY 2.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Muscat & Daymaniyat Islands

Jan.marine_life: Leopard sharks resting on sand, turtles, eagle rays, dense reef fish
Jan.conditions: Coolest water with thermoclines; mostly calm seas, clear winter viz

Feb.marine_life: Leopard sharks, honeycomb morays, rays, nudibranchs at their best
Feb.conditions: Chilly thermoclines below 15m; settled seas, good clarity

Mar.marine_life: Turtles gathering, leopard sharks, mobula rays start cruising through
Mar.conditions: Warming water, calm seas, visibility improving toward spring best

Apr.marine_life: Mobula squadrons, green and hawksbill turtles, big snapper schools
Apr.conditions: Flat seas and some of the clearest water of the year

May.marine_life: Turtles nesting on the islands, mobulas, cuttlefish, dense fish soup
May.conditions: Warm and calm; island landings close but diving carries on

Jun.marine_life: Plankton draws rays and the first big pelagic visitors
Jun.conditions: Very warm surface water; blooms start to green the viz

Jul.marine_life: Early whale shark chances, mobulas, baitballs in green water
Jul.conditions: Peak heat and plankton; viz drops but seas stay diveable

Aug.marine_life: Whale sharks feeding on blooms, devil rays, huge trevally schools
Aug.conditions: Hot and humid, milky plankton-rich water, occasional swell

Sep.marine_life: Whale shark aggregations, sometimes several per dive, plus mobulas
Sep.conditions: Blooms easing, viz rebuilding, warm calm seas

Oct.marine_life: Late whale sharks, mobulas, turtles, leopard sharks returning
Oct.conditions: Clearing fast; the sweet spot of big animals and good viz

Nov.marine_life: Mobula rays, eagle rays, turtles everywhere, thick reef life
Nov.conditions: Clear blue water, comfortable temps, reliably calm days

Dec.marine_life: Leopard sharks, turtles, morays, seahorses and macro on the reefs
Dec.conditions: Cooling water and first thermoclines; clean winter visibility

destinations.highlights: A protected island nature reserve an hour off Muscat where whale sharks gather over pristine hard coral in September-October, leopard sharks and turtles laze on the sand year-round, and an intact 84m navy wreck waits in a sheltered bay nearby.
destinations.currents: Currents around the Daymaniyats are generally gentle, with occasional moderate flow along exposed walls and over the Al Munassir wreck. Most sites are relaxed plateau and bay dives suited to easy multilevel profiles.
destinations.current_note: mostly mild; occasional pushes on walls and the wreck
destinations.wetsuit: 3mm full May-Oct; 5mm Nov-Apr for winter thermoclines

dive_sites.name: Aquarium
dive_sites.blurb: An oval coral plateau dropping to 30m, packed with leopard sharks, honeycomb morays, turtles and clouds of snapper.

dive_sites.name: Police Wall
dive_sites.blurb: A drop-off on Police Island where whale sharks and mobulas cruise past divers in season.

dive_sites.name: Three Sisters
dive_sites.blurb: Boulder swim-throughs draped in soft tree corals, reliable for eagle rays, leopard sharks and both turtle species.

dive_sites.name: Al Munassir Wreck
dive_sites.blurb: An 84m navy landing ship scuttled in 2003, sitting upright and intact with guns in place, penetrable and thick with morays.

dive_sites.name: Fahal Island
dive_sites.blurb: Muscat's 'shark island' with a swim-through cavern, blacktip reef sharks, rays and passing dolphins.

dive_sites.name: Bandar Khayran
dive_sites.blurb: Sheltered fjord-like bays of coral gardens ideal for easy dives, night dives and macro hunting.

destinations.description: An hour's boat ride from Oman's capital, the Daymaniyat Islands are a strictly protected reserve where Gulf of Oman nutrients meet genuinely healthy hard coral. Winter brings clear water, leopard sharks and turtles; summer plankton blooms trade visibility for whale sharks that peak in September and October, with mobula squadrons either side. Add Fahal Island and the sheltered wreck-filled bays of Bandar Khayran, and it suits any diver who values animals over infinity-pool viz.
destinations.image_credit: Photo: "dronepicr" (CC BY 2.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Oahu

Jan.marine_life: Humpback whale song on every dive, green turtles, whitetip reef sharks, eagle rays on the wrecks
Jan.conditions: Big winter swell shuts the North Shore; south-shore boat diving holds up, viz dips after rain

Feb.marine_life: Peak humpback season audibly underwater, turtles at cleaning stations, spinner dolphins along the west side
Feb.conditions: Coolest water of the year, large north swells, sheltered south and west shores diveable

Mar.marine_life: Last of the humpbacks, Galapagos and sandbar sharks offshore, octopus and frogfish on the reefs
Mar.conditions: North swell easing late month; passing fronts can stir shallow sites

Apr.marine_life: Turtles, eagle rays and schooling bluestripe snapper thick on the YO-257 and Sea Tiger
Apr.conditions: Swell windows open, seas settling, water warming and viz improving

May.marine_life: Green turtles everywhere, spinner dolphin pods, endemic reef fish, whitetips resting in lava tubes
May.conditions: North Shore shore sites open, calm seas island-wide, clean blue water

Jun.marine_life: Turtles, spinner dolphins, Galapagos sharks on offshore trips, dense reef-fish activity
Jun.conditions: Flat summer seas, steady trade winds, excellent visibility on all coasts

Jul.marine_life: Sharks Cove lava tubes busy with turtles, octopus and eels; tiger sharks patrol offshore banks
Jul.conditions: Warmest, calmest stretch; long flat spells and 25m-plus viz days

Aug.marine_life: Green turtles at cleaning stations, spotted eagle rays, spinner dolphins at Kahe Point
Aug.conditions: Warm water, gentle seas, all four shores routinely diveable

Sep.marine_life: Peak fish abundance, turtles, reef sharks and rays on wrecks, occasional monk seal sighting
Sep.conditions: Often the calmest, clearest month of the year across the island

Oct.marine_life: Turtles and eagle rays on the Waikiki wrecks, Galapagos sharks offshore, spinner dolphins westside
Oct.conditions: First north swells arrive late month; south shore stays reliable

Nov.marine_life: First humpbacks arriving, turtles, whitetips and big schools on the south-shore wrecks
Nov.conditions: North Shore closes as winter surf builds; boat diving off Waikiki still consistent

Dec.marine_life: Humpback whales returning in numbers, turtles, sandbar and Galapagos sharks offshore
Dec.conditions: Winter swell and rain squalls; diving retreats to the sheltered south and west shores

destinations.highlights: Two intact wrecks minutes off Waikiki, summer lava-tube diving on the North Shore, green turtles at every cleaning station and pelagic sharks on the offshore banks.
destinations.currents: Most reef sites see mild tidal flow, but the offshore wrecks off Waikiki can carry noticeable current on the mooring lines, and winter swell adds strong surge to shallow shore sites. Channels between coasts and the offshore shark banks run stronger.
destinations.current_note: mild on reefs; current and surge on wrecks and in winter

dive_sites.name: YO-257 & San Pedro
dive_sites.blurb: Twin wrecks off Waikiki sunk as artificial reefs, swarming with turtles, eagle rays and schooling snapper a short boat ride from Honolulu.

dive_sites.name: Sea Tiger
dive_sites.blurb: Largely intact former trading vessel with open swim-throughs, resident turtles and whitetips at the edge of recreational depth.

dive_sites.name: Sharks Cove
dive_sites.blurb: North Shore lava tubes and caverns that only open in summer flat spells, packed with turtles, octopus and endemic reef fish.

dive_sites.name: Corsair
dive_sites.blurb: A WWII-era fighter plane sitting upright on a bare sand flat, ringed by garden eels and occasional passing eagle rays.

dive_sites.name: Kahe Point (Electric Beach)
dive_sites.blurb: Warm-water outflow on the west side that draws crowds of fish, green turtles and regular spinner dolphin fly-bys.

dive_sites.name: Makaha Caverns
dive_sites.blurb: Collapsed lava-tube network on the leeward coast with skylights, resting whitetip sharks and reliably easy conditions.

dive_sites.name: Hanauma Bay Witches Brew
dive_sites.blurb: Outer corner of the protected crater bay where dense, unafraid fish life and turtle traffic reward a longer swim.

destinations.description: Oahu packs real variety into one very reachable island: intact wrecks like the YO-257 and Sea Tiger minutes off Waikiki, lava-tube caverns at Sharks Cove and Makaha, and shark banks offshore where Galapagos and tiger sharks patrol. Green turtles are near-guaranteed, spinner dolphins escort west-side boats, and winter dives come with humpback song. South and west shores dive year-round; the North Shore is a summer-only prize when the surf sleeps.
destinations.image_credit: Photo: nick hoke (CC BY 3.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Phuket

Jan.marine_life: Leopard sharks on the sand at Shark Point, manta sightings at Racha Noi, dense reef fish everywhere
Jan.conditions: Dry-season calm, flat crossings to all local sites, excellent viz

Feb.marine_life: Mantas cruising Racha Noi's south tip, leopard and blacktip reef sharks, big barracuda schools
Feb.conditions: Calmest seas of the year, light winds, superb viz

Mar.marine_life: Manta chances continue, turtles and leopard sharks, plankton pulses can pull in big filter feeders
Mar.conditions: Warm, glassy seas; viz occasionally hazy with plankton

Apr.marine_life: Last reliable manta window at Racha Noi, leopard sharks, huge snapper and fusilier schools
Apr.conditions: Hottest month, seas still mostly calm before the monsoon turns

May.marine_life: Reef life unchanged — leopard sharks, turtles, moray-rich pinnacles
May.conditions: Southwest monsoon arrives; passing squalls, crossings get lumpier

Jun.marine_life: Healthy reef action at the Rachas, bamboo sharks in the King Cruiser, macro on Koh Doc Mai
Jun.conditions: Green season; boats favor sheltered east-coast Racha sites, viz variable

Jul.marine_life: Racha Yai's east bays fish-thick; leopard sharks still around Shark Point on calm days
Jul.conditions: Monsoon swell and rain in bursts; outer-site trips weather-dependent

Aug.marine_life: Reef staples remain — turtles, morays, schooling snapper in protected bays
Aug.conditions: Rough windward seas and rain; some crossings cancelled, reduced viz

Sep.marine_life: Marine life unchanged but harder to reach; macro life thrives in the nutrient-rich water
Sep.conditions: Wettest month, biggest swell, most cancellations; sheltered sites only

Oct.marine_life: Fish stocks fattened by monsoon nutrients, leopard sharks returning to the pinnacles
Oct.conditions: Monsoon tapering; seas settle late in the month, viz improving

Nov.marine_life: First mantas of the season at Racha Noi, leopard sharks, thick soft-coral pinnacles in full color
Nov.conditions: Dry season begins; seas flatten quickly, viz climbs

Dec.marine_life: Mantas, leopard and blacktip sharks, turtles, barracuda tornadoes at the Bidas
Dec.conditions: Prime dry-season diving, calm crossings to every local site

destinations.highlights: Easy day-boat diving from a full-service island hub: leopard sharks and soft-coral pinnacles at Shark Point, the 85m King Cruiser wreck, manta chances at Racha Noi, and Phi Phi's limestone walls all within a day's reach.
destinations.currents: Tidal currents run north-south across the mid-channel pinnacles and can be brisk at Shark Point, Anemone Reef and the King Cruiser, especially around spring tides. The Racha Islands are gentler, though Racha Noi's exposed tips can pump when the manta action is best.
destinations.current_note: strongest at the mid-channel pinnacles and wreck on spring tides
destinations.wetsuit: 3mm shorty or full suit year-round; water rarely drops below 28°C

dive_sites.name: Shark Point (Hin Mu Sang)
dive_sites.blurb: Three soft-coral-smothered limestone pinnacles famous for leopard sharks resting on the sand.

dive_sites.name: Anemone Reef (Hin Jom)
dive_sites.blurb: A submerged pinnacle carpeted in sea anemones that pulls in hunting trevally, tuna and barracuda.

dive_sites.name: King Cruiser Wreck
dive_sites.blurb: An 85m car ferry sunk in 1997 after striking Anemone Reef, now thick with schooling snapper, lionfish and bamboo sharks.

dive_sites.name: Racha Noi South Tip
dive_sites.blurb: Granite boulders and a cleaning station with Phuket's most reliable oceanic manta encounters in season.

dive_sites.name: Racha Yai Bay 1
dive_sites.blurb: Sheltered east-coast hard-coral slopes with reliably clear water, ideal for training and easy multi-dive days.

dive_sites.name: Koh Doc Mai
dive_sites.blurb: A limestone islet whose sheer wall of sea fans and oysters hides seahorses, ghost pipefish and other prized macro.

dive_sites.name: Koh Bida Nok (Phi Phi)
dive_sites.blurb: Phi Phi's signature limestone karst dive, mixing soft-coral walls and overhangs with blacktip reef sharks and turtles.

destinations.description: Phuket is Thailand's day-trip diving workhorse: big comfortable boats leave Chalong every morning for the granite boulders of the Racha Islands, the leopard-shark pinnacles of Shark Point and Anemone Reef, the King Cruiser wreck, and Phi Phi's karst walls. Warm, easy water and sheltered training bays suit new divers, while the wreck, currents and Racha Noi's manta-patrolled south tip give experienced divers real teeth. November to April delivers the calm seas and best visibility.

destinations.name: Santorini

Jan.marine_life: resident groupers, morays and octopus hunkered in the lava rock
Jan.conditions: cool water, winter winds and swell; most operators closed

Feb.marine_life: octopus, morays and nudibranchs on quiet reefs
Feb.conditions: coldest water of the year; exposed caldera sites often blown out

Mar.marine_life: nudibranchs and crustaceans active in cold clear water
Mar.conditions: cold and windy late winter; boats rarely running

Apr.marine_life: reef fish waking up; wrasse, groupers and early octopus activity
Apr.conditions: season opening; water still chilly, viz sharpening

May.marine_life: groupers, morays, octopus and busy wrasse over the lava walls
May.conditions: settling seas and empty sites; cool water in a thick suit

Jun.marine_life: anthias clouds, barracuda schooling, octopus and nudibranchs everywhere
Jun.conditions: warm, calm and glass-clear inside the caldera

Jul.marine_life: barracuda and tuna passing offshore; glassfish packing the caverns
Jul.conditions: warmest, calmest diving; meltemi gusts topside but sheltered caldera

Aug.marine_life: peak fish activity — barracuda, groupers, rays and dense baitfish
Aug.conditions: hottest water of the year; superb viz, busiest boats

Sep.marine_life: big groupers out in the open, hunting barracuda and pelagic visitors
Sep.conditions: warm sea, calming winds and thinning crowds — prime month

Oct.marine_life: groupers, morays and octopus still active as water cools
Oct.conditions: quiet end of season; still-warm water, first autumn swells

Nov.marine_life: resident reef life only; octopus and cuttlefish on the black sand
Nov.conditions: operators winding down; increasing wind and swell

Dec.marine_life: sparse winter reef life sheltering in the volcanic rock
Dec.conditions: cool rough winter sea; diving effectively shut down

destinations.highlights: Diving inside a flooded volcanic caldera on sheer lava walls, the shallow Santa Maria wreck off Nea Kameni and glass-clear Aegean water with 30m-plus visibility.
destinations.currents: The Aegean is virtually tideless and the caldera is sheltered, so water movement is minimal to non-existent at most sites. Outer-coast walls can pick up light surface chop and mild drift when the summer meltemi blows.
destinations.current_note: essentially still water inside the caldera
destinations.wetsuit: 3mm or shorty in Jul-Aug; 5mm for most of the season; 7mm in May, Oct and winter

dive_sites.name: Santa Maria Wreck
dive_sites.blurb: A 35m passenger ship sunk off Nea Kameni in the 1970s, sitting shallow enough for any certified diver and thick with glassfish and morays.

dive_sites.name: Nea Kameni Volcanic Reef
dive_sites.blurb: Lava flows and black volcanic boulders around the caldera's active young islet, with a WWII boat and a deeper tug lying nearby.

dive_sites.name: Adiavati Reef
dive_sites.blurb: A reef inside the caldera rim — locally tied to the Atlantis legend — crawling with crustaceans, invertebrates, groupers and barracuda.

dive_sites.name: Aspronisi (White Island) Walls
dive_sites.blurb: Sponge-encrusted lava walls on the caldera's western edge that start at snorkel depth and plunge past 40m into deep blue.

dive_sites.name: The Caves
dive_sites.blurb: Shallow volcanic caverns, canyons and swim-throughs on the south coast, lit by shafts of sun and packed with glassfish and octopus.

dive_sites.name: Perissa Rock
dive_sites.blurb: Maze-like volcanic rock formations off the black-sand beach, an easy classroom reef full of wrasse, morays and crustaceans.

destinations.description: Santorini is diving inside a drowned volcano: sheer lava walls plunge from the caldera rim into deep cobalt water, and the shallow Santa Maria wreck rests off the still-steaming islet of Nea Kameni. Life is classic Mediterranean — groupers, morays, octopus, passing barracuda — sparse but sharply lit by 30m visibility and near-zero current. Best for divers who value geology, wrecks and effortless conditions over fish density, from easy shore reefs to deep caldera walls.
destinations.image_credit: Photo: Andreas Pantziarides (CC BY 3.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Tenerife

Jan.marine_life: Angel sharks on the sand, stingrays gathering, turtles at El Puertito
Jan.conditions: Coolest water but crisp winter viz; south coast stays sheltered

Feb.marine_life: Peak angel shark encounters, rays pairing up in the caves, morays and octopus
Feb.conditions: Cool and clear; occasional Atlantic swell on exposed corners

Mar.marine_life: Last reliable angel sharks, stingrays, cuttlefish and trumpetfish everywhere
Mar.conditions: Clear late-winter water, seas settling as spring arrives

Apr.marine_life: Angel sharks thinning out; turtles, barracuda schools and reef fish carry the show
Apr.conditions: Transitional; water still cool, generally calm with good viz

May.marine_life: Turtles, bull rays cruising the drop-offs, big schools of bream and roncadores
May.conditions: Calm spring seas, warming water, reliably clear

Jun.marine_life: Rays active, turtles daily at El Puertito, barracuda and bastard grunt schools
Jun.conditions: Warm, settled early summer; trade winds pick up above water

Jul.marine_life: Turtles, stingrays, trumpetfish clouds; juveniles fill the volcanic reef
Jul.conditions: Warm and busy; brief upwellings can trim viz on some days

Aug.marine_life: Stingray aggregations off Las Galletas, turtles, octopus and cuttlefish
Aug.conditions: Warmest air, lively resorts; sea calm with occasional plankton haze

Sep.marine_life: Warmest-water action: bull rays, big barracuda, turtles and dense fish schools
Sep.conditions: Sea at its warmest and calmest; long settled spells

Oct.marine_life: Rays and turtles everywhere, roncador schools thick over the wrecks
Oct.conditions: Warm water lingers, glassy autumn seas, excellent viz

Nov.marine_life: Stingrays massing, turtles, first angel sharks edging into the shallows
Nov.conditions: Still-warm water, quiet sites, clear autumn conditions

Dec.marine_life: Angel shark season opens; rays, turtles and cuttlefish on the sand flats
Dec.conditions: Mild winter, sheltered south coast diving, viz sharpening

destinations.highlights: Volcanic Atlantic diving off Europe's doorstep — green turtles over the El Puertito seagrass, stingray aggregations off Las Galletas, the basalt-column Condesito wreck, and one of the world's few reliable winter windows on critically endangered angel sharks.
destinations.currents: The leeward south coast is well sheltered from the prevailing northeast trades, so most sites see little to no current. Exposed headlands and offshore banks can pick up moderate flow when Atlantic swell or spring tides run.
destinations.current_note: Mostly calm on the sheltered south coast
destinations.wetsuit: 5mm full year-round; add a hooded vest or 7mm Dec-Apr, 3mm fine Aug-Oct

dive_sites.name: El Puertito
dive_sites.blurb: Sheltered sandy bay near Armeñime where resident green turtles graze the seagrass at snoozing-pace depths.

dive_sites.name: El Condesito
dive_sites.blurb: Cement carrier lost in 1971, its scattered hull draped against hexagonal basalt columns and patrolled by trumpetfish and morays.

dive_sites.name: Los Chuchos
dive_sites.blurb: The 'city of stingrays' minutes off Las Galletas harbour, with overhangs, a small sunken fishing boat and the island's densest ray population.

dive_sites.name: Montaña Amarilla
dive_sites.blurb: Shore-access lunar landscape of yellow volcanic tuff with terraces, swim-throughs and winter angel shark sightings.

dive_sites.name: Tabaiba Wreck (El Peñón)
dive_sites.blurb: Intact 35m tug scuttled off Tabaiba, upright at 30m with engine-room penetration for advanced divers.

dive_sites.name: Palm-Mar Cave
dive_sites.blurb: Galleried lava cave below a 17m plateau where Atlantic stingrays gather to court in winter.

destinations.description: Tenerife turns volcanic geology into a year-round Atlantic playground: basalt columns, lava arches and yellow tuff terraces in water that never leaves the high teens. Green turtles hold court at El Puertito, stingrays mass off Las Galletas, and from December to March the sand hides critically endangered angel sharks — one of the few places on earth you can count on them. Easy shore entries and short boat rides suit new divers; wrecks and lava caves keep experienced hands busy.

destinations.name: Zanzibar

Jan.marine_life: whale sharks passing offshore, turtles thick on Mnemba, dolphins in the channel
Jan.conditions: kaskazi monsoon, warm calm seas, superb visibility

Feb.marine_life: reef fish spawning aggregations, green turtles, resident bottlenose dolphins
Feb.conditions: hot and settled, glassy mornings, top-of-year visibility

Mar.marine_life: turtles, Napoleon wrasse and late-season whale sharks before the rains
Mar.conditions: calmest seas of the year as kaskazi fades, warmest water

Apr.marine_life: reef life unchanged but sightings limited by runoff and plankton
Apr.conditions: long rains (masika), heavy downpours, reduced visibility, some boats pause

May.marine_life: macro on the reefs for those who go; big animals scarce
May.conditions: tail end of the long rains, choppy seas, patchy visibility

Jun.marine_life: healthy reef action returns, turtles and schooling trevally at Leven Bank
Jun.conditions: dry season begins, kusi breeze, cooling clearing water

Jul.marine_life: pelagics on the northern banks, dense reef fish at Mnemba
Jul.conditions: kusi monsoon wind, some chop on crossings, good visibility

Aug.marine_life: first humpback whales migrate past Leven Bank and the Mnemba channel
Aug.conditions: breezy kusi peak, coolest water, reliable visibility

Sep.marine_life: humpback whales at their peak, barracuda and tuna schooling offshore
Sep.conditions: winds easing, dry and clear, excellent visibility

Oct.marine_life: late humpbacks overlap with the first whale sharks; turtles everywhere
Oct.conditions: calm inter-monsoon lull, flat seas, superb visibility

Nov.marine_life: whale shark season builds, dolphins and rays in the channels
Nov.conditions: short rains (vuli) bring brief showers, seas mostly workable

Dec.marine_life: whale sharks most frequent, turtles nesting-season active at Mnemba
Dec.conditions: kaskazi sets in, warm water, visibility climbing again

destinations.highlights: Mnemba Atoll's turtle-packed coral slopes and the current-swept pelagic seamount of Leven Bank, with humpback whales passing in September and whale sharks from October to February.
destinations.currents: Tides of up to five metres drive currents that range from slack to brisk on the Mnemba and Tumbatu reefs, making gentle drifts the norm. Leven Bank in the open Pemba Channel is another matter, with strong, unpredictable current that demands drift experience.
destinations.current_note: gentle reef drifts inshore; strong and shifty at Leven Bank
destinations.wetsuit: 3mm shorty or full suit Nov-Apr; 3mm-5mm full in the cooler Jun-Sep season

dive_sites.name: Kichwani
dive_sites.blurb: Mnemba's classic sloping wall drift, dense with hard and soft coral, trevally, morays and well-camouflaged leaf fish.

dive_sites.name: Wattabomi
dive_sites.blurb: A gentle Mnemba slope of bommies and plate coral where green turtles are near-guaranteed and dolphins sometimes cruise through mid-dive.

dive_sites.name: Big Wall
dive_sites.blurb: Mnemba's vertical face of overhangs and sponge-crusted ledges, drawing tuna, barracuda, rays and the occasional hammerhead off the blue.

dive_sites.name: Leven Bank
dive_sites.blurb: An open-ocean seamount in the Pemba Channel where strong current pulls in kingfish, tuna, big morays and schooling barracuda.

dive_sites.name: Hunga Reef
dive_sites.blurb: A circular open-water reef off Nungwi known for huge coral bommies and consistently sharp visibility.

dive_sites.name: Tumbatu Coral Gardens
dive_sites.blurb: Quiet, current-brushed gardens off Tumbatu Island regarded as Zanzibar's healthiest coral, with seahorses and turtles among the highlights.

destinations.description: Zanzibar pairs easy tropical reef diving with a genuine big-animal calendar. Day boats work Mnemba Atoll's coral slopes, where green turtles and dolphins are routine, while Leven Bank's current-raked seamount gives experienced divers kingfish, tuna and barracuda in the Pemba Channel. Two monsoons shape the year: kaskazi brings warm, glassy water from December to March, kusi cooler humpback-season seas from June to October, and the April-May long rains close the window. Suits new divers and drift-hungry veterans alike.
